periods.market_context: In September, the yield on the 10-year US Treasury bond reached 4.8%, its highest level since 2007, precipitating weakness across asset classes. Expectations shifted to rates staying above 5% until September 2024. There's an emerging acceptance that the 'Goldilocks world' has given way to a more bearish reality, with the Federal Reserve committed to keeping rates high. Equity valuations fell, impacting 'bond proxies'. The consensus earnings growth for most listed companies doesn't account for a recession, despite historical patterns showing recessions often follow rate hiking cycles with a lag. Businesses, particularly small ones, face financial distress due to rising borrowing costs (4% to 10%) and tightening lending standards by regional US banks.
periods.thesis: We are sceptical that 'immaculate disinflation' can be achieved without higher rates impacting growth. We believe rates will remain structurally higher and inflation higher and less stable in the future. Our view on higher rates means the price paid for investments is crucial. We anticipate opportunities to buy at lower valuations will coincide with earnings deterioration. We maintain conviction in the longer-run durability of exceptional business models, making us optimistic about building equity exposure. We also see persistent opportunities in digitisation (cloud computing, digital payments, AI) and the growing wealth of Asian consumers.
periods.decisions_taken: As real yields rose, we added to our index-linked holdings. Specifically, in August, we initiated holdings in US and UK 2026-maturing bonds at positive real yields of over 2.5% and over 1.5% respectively. (Prior to this, we had not owned UK index-linked bonds since 2019). We are optimistic about building on the current 25% portfolio allocation to equities.
periods.observed_outcome: The portfolio generated modestly positive returns year-to-date. The index-linked bond asset class overall was around flat in terms of contribution to returns, which was 'a little disappointing' but expected given rising interest rate expectations. However, most shorter-dated index-linked bonds in the portfolio generated a positive absolute return, largely due to inflation accrual (principal value rising as inflation remained strong), which offset the headwind of rising real yields.

extracted_from: We remain sceptical that such an immaculate disinflation can be achieved without higher rates having a derailing impact on growth. As real yields have risen, we have added to our index-linked holdings.
extraction_notes: The document discusses the 'Troy Multi-Asset Strategy'. It is assumed this strategy applies to the target fund, Trojan Fund (Ireland) (ISIN IE00B6T42S66), as the report is from Troy Asset Management and lists the same managers.
